content: Lydia: By Dibella's dripping cunt, this marsh reeks worse than a skeever's asshole after a week of venison stew. You serve any decent mead here, or just swamp water and regret? Jonna: Heh. Ain't no fancy Whiterun honeybrew here, but the ale's strong enough to make a bard sound good. What'll it be, housecarl? Lydia: A barrel of whatever won't make me puke when I skewer those bandit sluts trying to ride my Thane raw later. You ever had to gut a whore before she could suck your man dry? Jonna: Can't say I have, though I did stab a drunk Breton who tried to grab my tits last winter. Blade went right through his palm—served as a good lesson for the rest. Lydia: Fuck yeah. Men need to learn their place—face down or on their knees. Unless they're my Thane. That man can bend me over this bar and split me like firewood if he pleases. Jonna: Keep that talk up and you'll scare off the few patrons I got left. Then again, maybe that's for the best—last thing I need is Lurbuk getting ideas about writing a ballad about your love life. Lydia: Ugh, that talentless shitstain still here? Bet his voice cracks harder than a virgin's hymen at an orgy. I'd pay in septims to watch a gargoyle rip out his tongue. Jonna: Heh. Tempting, but gold's gold. Speaking of—your Thane looks like he could afford to buy this whole piss-puddle town. Stormcloak gold or Imperial bribes? Lydia: Stormcloak, obviously. True Nords don't lick Elven boots. Skyrim belongs to the strong—men who fuck hard and fight harder. Unlike these milk-drinking Morthal swamp rats, whining about mages while their Jarl spouts nonsense. Jonna: Careful. nonsense

Lydia: Aye, enough politics. I’d rather focus on what matters—like keeping my Thane’s cock warm and his enemies’ heads rolling. Toss me that ale. Jonna: Here. Try not to break anything. Or do—just tip extra if you do. Lydia: You’re alright, Redguard. For a foreigner. Now shut up and pour before I start comparing your brother to the Thalmor again. Jonna: Ha! Fair enough. Drink in peace, then. Or don’t. Up to you.
